How SAM4 Detects Equipment Faults From the Electrical Cabinet

One sensor per motor. No production downtime. Faults detected 6–8 weeks before failure.

Request a Demo Explore the Science
96%
Detection Accuracy
<1%
False Alarm Rate
200+
Industrial Sites
10,000+
Assets Monitored

Why Traditional Monitoring Falls Short

Most industrial plants monitor fewer than half their rotating assets. The rest — submerged pumps, remote fans, ATEX-zone compressors — run unmonitored until they fail. That gap costs the average plant 3–5% of annual production value in unplanned downtime.

Manual routes miss developing faults

Monthly vibration walks capture a snapshot. Faults that develop between visits go undetected until they become emergencies.

Vibration requires physical access

Sensors need line-of-sight to the asset. Submerged, enclosed, or hazardous-zone equipment often goes unmonitored.

Time-based maintenance wastes resources

Replacing parts on a schedule means replacing working components and missing the ones that fail early. Neither outcome is efficient.

The 4-Step Monitoring Process

From sensor installation to maintenance alert — no production interruption at any stage.

1
Install
A compact sensor is placed in the Motor Control Cabinet (MCC). No production downtime. Under 5 minutes per asset.
2
Capture
The sensor captures continuous electrical current waveforms — voltage, current, and harmonic content — streaming data in real time.
3
Analyse
SAM4's AI decomposes the electrical signature into frequency components. It matches patterns against 50,000+ validated fault examples. 96% detection accuracy.
4
Act
Automated alerts route to your maintenance team via SAP PM, Maximo, or other CMMS. Each alert includes fault type, severity, and recommended action.

The Science Behind ESA

Every AC motor draws electrical current that carries information about its mechanical condition. When a bearing degrades, the increased friction modulates the motor's current waveform. When an impeller clogs, the load change shifts the harmonic spectrum.

Electrical Signature Analysis (ESA) reads these changes. SAM4 decomposes the raw waveform into its frequency components — the same principle as a prism splitting white light into colours. Each fault type produces a distinct frequency pattern. Bearing wear raises sidebands at 3–5 times running speed. Unbalance amplifies the fundamental frequency. Looseness creates broadband spectral noise.

SAM4's AI model has been trained on over 50,000 real-world, field-confirmed equipment failures. It matches incoming patterns against this validated library — detecting faults 4–12 weeks before catastrophic failure.

Dive Deeper Into the Physics →

The Electrical Transmission Path

Mechanical faults propagate back through the drivetrain and modulate the motor's electrical signature. SAM4 reads these modulations from the MCC — without touching the equipment.

Grid / VFD
Power supply quality, VFD harmonics
Motor
Winding insulation, rotor bar defects, phase imbalance
Transmission
Belt slip, gearbox wear, coupling misalignment
Load
Bearing degradation, unbalance, cavitation, clogging

Every fault along this chain modulates the electrical current. SAM4 reads these changes at the MCC — no sensors on the equipment itself.

What ESA Detects

Each fault type produces a distinct electrical fingerprint. SAM4 identifies and classifies these patterns continuously.

Bearing Inner Race Degradation
Full

Current harmonic distortion rises progressively as inner race spalling develops. Detectable 4–12 weeks before catastrophic failure.

Unbalance
Full

Mass imbalance amplifies the fundamental running frequency in the electrical signature. Reliable detection across all load conditions.

Misalignment
Full

Angular or parallel misalignment produces characteristic 2× running frequency harmonics in both current and vibration spectra.

Soft Foot
Full

Mounting stress distorts the motor frame, creating detectable asymmetry in the electrical signature. Often missed by vibration.

Looseness
Full

Mechanical looseness generates broadband spectral noise and sub-harmonic content. Detectable at early stages before vibration shows clear symptoms.

Belt Slip
Conditional

Slip creates periodic load fluctuations visible in the electrical signature. Detection depends on belt type and drive configuration.

Cavitation (Pumps)
Full

Cavitation changes the hydraulic load on the impeller, producing distinct current amplitude fluctuations at specific frequencies.

Clogging (Pumps)
Full

Progressive clogging shifts the operating point on the pump curve, creating measurable changes in power draw and harmonic content.

How does ESA compare to vibration monitoring?

ESA and vibration are complementary. See the side-by-side comparison — coverage, cost, installation, accuracy — to decide where each fits.

View the comparison →

Works With Your Systems

SAM4 integrates with the tools your maintenance and operations teams already use. No rip-and-replace.

CMMS Integration

SAP PM, IBM Maximo, Infor EAM. Alerts create work orders automatically.

SCADA & Historians

OPC UA, MQTT, REST API. Feed diagnostic data into existing control systems.

OT/IT Security

Encrypted communication. No inbound network access. Cloud-first, cellular architecture. ISO 27001 certified.

Data Export

JSON, CSV. Full diagnostic history available via API for custom analytics and reporting.

Why This Matters to Your Role

Operations Leader
  • Continuous visibility across all rotating assets — no site visits required
  • Fewer emergency calls. Maintenance becomes planned, not reactive
  • False alarm rate below 1% — alerts you can trust and act on
  • Extends monitoring to submerged and hazardous-zone equipment
Maintenance Engineer
  • Early fault detection: 4–12 weeks before mechanical failure
  • Specific fault diagnosis — bearing wear, unbalance, cavitation — not just "anomaly"
  • Confidence to schedule maintenance without second-guessing
  • Integrates with your CMMS: work orders created automatically
Reliability / Innovation Leader
  • Data-driven maintenance strategy with measurable reliability improvement
  • Cost per detection significantly below cost of failure
  • Scalable: onboard 500+ assets without proportional headcount increase
  • Technology advantage over competitors still running time-based maintenance

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the minimum motor size SAM4 can monitor?

SAM4 monitors AC motors from 1 kW and above. The motor needs sufficient load — typically above 30% of rated load — for ESA to establish a reliable baseline.

Does ESA work with Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)?

Yes. SAM4 is VFD-optimised. The harmonic content introduced by VFDs actually improves fault detection by providing additional frequency markers for the AI model to analyse.

How is data transmitted from the sensor?

Cellular (4G LTE), Ethernet, or WiFi. All data is encrypted in transit and at rest. The architecture is cloud-first with no inbound network access required — only outbound connections.

What happens if the equipment stops running?

Data logging continues. The baseline adjusts to idle state. When equipment restarts, detection resumes automatically. No manual intervention needed.

Can ESA detect electrical faults in the motor itself?

Yes. ESA detects stator winding insulation degradation, phase imbalance, rotor bar defects, and other electrical anomalies — faults invisible to vibration sensors.

What is the false alarm rate?

Less than 1% in standard operating conditions. SAM4's AI model is trained on field-confirmed faults, which keeps false positives exceptionally low.

Can I retrofit ESA alongside existing vibration monitoring?

Yes. ESA operates on electrical signals; vibration sensors measure mechanical displacement. The two methods are independent and complementary. No interference.

How often should sensors be recalibrated?

Annual calibration is recommended. Baseline drift is minimal because electrical signatures are stable under consistent operating conditions.
